body_txt: A pilot is a conceptual demonstration of a series idea, filmed and assembled to give the network an idea of the actors involved, the stories that can be told, how it will look, pacing, themes and, via viewer testing, what Target Audience it will appeal to. On American TV, only about a quarter of pilots are turned into a series note Sometimes, however, they will throw it back to the producers and say, "try again" . Some insiders have snidely claimed that Hollywood is more about making pilots than actually making shows. Sometimes multiple pilots are made for the same original idea and you can see an evolution of actors, sets, and what the first story is about. Even when a show is picked up and given a timeslot, there is no guarantee that a pilot will ever reach the air. Granted, they usually do air, and as the premiere episode (for obvious reasons). But sometimes, usually with those shows whose producers were told "try again", the original pilot is so different from what reached the air that they don't try to use it. Most pilots fall into the category of the Welcome Episode or Everyone Meets Everyone . It'd make more sense to list the exceptions than the examples. They may also contain a First-Episode Twist . Live-action pilots often have somewhat larger budgets than a typical episode of the series, the price of getting cast, crew and locations for a one-off idea, but fewer purpose-built sets. A hospital or school or graveyard in a pilot is likely to be the real thing - no sense building an elaborate set for a pilot that probably won't be picked up. As such, if the series is picked up and purpose-built sets are built to replace these locations, then eagle-eyed viewers might be able to spot differences between the characters' base of operations from the first week to the second. When certain characters and concepts are removed after the pilot and not seen in subsequent episodes, that is Dropped After the Pilot . The writing is often front-loaded as introduction heavy and perhaps a little too much exposition. Revealing all the characters and setting up the situation in a limited time can be difficult to do in a natural way, and even the best pilots can be prone to clunky commentary in an effort to get it out of the way. Remember, this is not necessarily to build an audience but to sell the show to a network. They may be shaped differently when picked up, some are used entirely as a proof-of-concept for dialogue and action, meaning they may not even be the proper length and will be refilmed in its' entirety with the official script. Pilots may also be filmed on a different stock than the rest of the series; alternating between looking more 'cinematic' or stylistically rougher in story and cinematography than other episodes in the series. A Pilot Movie is usually trying to sell an idea through a standalone Made-for-TV Movie . The same rules roughly apply to animation, except that a pilot in that industry usually never exceeds eleven minutes. Movies themselves are often sold on test footage both to show what the movie will look like but also to convince them that the production team has what it takes to pull it off. Note that a pilot episode is a term used for marketing a show to a network, this does not happen with every show. When there is an established franchise or a sufficiently powerful producer involved a show may get a straight episode order and no pilot is made, it goes straight into the story and there isn't a strain to make the first episode all-encompassing. This makes the initial batch of episodes a bit more relaxed, allowing characters to be developed later on rather than be in a rush to outline everything. This has become more common in the streaming era, in large part due to services looking to fill their library as fast as possible. Many, many pilot episodes are simply named "Pilot", making "Pilot" the most common episode title among all series. Sometimes a pilot idea is integrated into another series in a form of piggybacking production time and actors, which is a Poorly Disguised Pilot . See Failed Pilot Episode for an index of pilots that have pages but not full shows. The term "pilot" is used in a similar context outside the entertainment industry; a "pilot plant" is a smaller-scale power plant that's used to test some new generation technology. Not to be confused with the webcomic of the same name .
